title: Emergent Self-Similar Quantum Revivals in Spiral Drives
authors: Xin-Chi Zhou, Liang-Hong Mo, Hongzheng Zhao, Bastien Lapierre
abstract: We uncover a distinct form of nonequilibrium temporal order: self-similar quantum revivals in a many-body system driven by quasiperiodic spiral kicks, where the system recurrently returns close to its initial state at a hierarchically nested sequence of times. We demonstrate that both the fidelity and entanglement entropy exhibit this self-similar temporal structure. It originates from an emergent dynamical attractor, which we identify, such that all momentum modes eventually fall into the same closed orbits at self-similar times. We analytically justify this behavior and show that, for special momentum modes, this attractor arises as a consequence of a generalized spin echo process, and more generally we prove its existence using quasiperiodic SU(2) cocycles. Interestingly, the dynamics between consecutive revivals supports either volume- or area-law entanglement scaling, tunable via the driving parameters. In the presence of integrability-breaking perturbations, the system eventually heats up, but a long-lived prethermal regime with algebraically tunable lifetime occurs before heating sets in. Our results establish self-similar quantum revivals as a new paradigm for nonequilibrium quantum matter and provide a realistic route for its observation in current quantum simulators.
summary: The paper introduces the concept of self-similar quantum revivals as a novel form of temporal order in non-equilibrium quantum matter. By analyzing systems driven by quasiperiodic spiral kicks, the authors prove that both fidelity and entanglement entropy exhibit hierarchical recurrence patterns. This work establishes a new theoretical framework for understanding memory retention and dynamics far from equilibrium.
main_problem: To investigate whether self-similarity can emerge as a fundamental organizing principle of far-from-equilibrium quantum matter in the time domain.
main_result: The authors uncovered a distinct form of nonequilibrium temporal order: self-similar quantum revivals, which are robustly observed in both fidelity and entanglement entropy for systems driven by quasiperiodic spiral kicks.
method: The analysis combines analytical proofs using quasiperiodic cocycles over SU(2) with numerical simulations tracking state fidelity and bipartite entanglement entropy across different driving parameters.
model_or_system: The model is a many-body system (Ising chain) subjected to time evolution governed by quasiperiodic spiral kicks, which are constructed from an arbitrary non-diagonal SU(2) matrix combined with $\sigma_z$ gates. The dynamics are analyzed in momentum space.
key_observables: State fidelity $F(n)$, half-chain bipartite Entanglement Entropy $S_{L/2}(n)$, and the convergence of the product of transfer matrices to a period-6 cycle.
important_parameters: The quasiperiodic driving angle $\varphi$ (related to the golden ratio), perturbation strength ($	heta_z$), and entanglement thresholds ($S$).
main_assumptions: The mathematical derivations often rely on assuming that the underlying quasiperiodic cocycle is reducible, which simplifies the analysis significantly.
figures_summary: Figures show the revival structure in $F(n)$ and $S_{L/2}(n)$ at specific times ($n=F^{3m}$), the convergence of momentum mode trajectories to an attractor, and the scaling of prethermal lifetime $	au$ with perturbation strength.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the general concept of self-similar revivals in integrable settings, providing analytical justifications via cocycles, characterizing entanglement growth regimes (area/volume law), and finally demonstrating robustness against generic integrability-breaking perturbations to establish a new paradigm for non-equilibrium quantum matter.
why_interesting: This work provides concrete theoretical predictions about emergent temporal order in driven many-body systems, offering testable signatures (self-similar revivals) that can be sought in current quantum simulators using superconducting qubits or trapped ions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. (a) Circuit representation of the spiral drive introduced in Eq. (1). Different colors for the X gates indicate different irrational single-qubit rotations, following θn = nπφ (mod 2π). These successive rotations form a characteristic Fermat’s spiral. (b) Time evolution of the fidelity F(n) and half-system entanglement entropy SL/2(n) as a function of the stroboscopic step n, for L = 1000, and ϕz = π/3. The fidelity reveals near revivals at special times, highlighted in orange, corresponding to n = F3m. Similar features can be observed in SL/2(n), which grows logarithmically with n before eventually saturating to low-entanglement states. A magnified view of SL/2(n′) starting at n′...

Figure 3. Parametrically long-lived non-heating dynamics under ran- dom integrability-breaking. (a) The bipartite entanglement entropy SL/2(n) at Fibonacci times n = Fm (filled circles) and at loga- rithmically spaced intermediate times (light dots) for different noise strengths θz. A prethermal regime emerges, in which the non-heating dynamics persists, as evidenced by revivals toward states close to the initial state. Shaded bands indicate ± one standard deviation over 10 random realizations, and the dashed line marks the Page value. (b) Prethermal lifetime τ versus θz. Here τ is defined as the time at which SL/2(n) exceeds the threshold Sc L/2 = 8. The fitted scaling τ ∼θ−2 z (dashed...

Figure 2. (a) The bipartite entanglement entropy SL/2(n) for the spiral drive compared with three reference protocols: the random drive with θn sampled uniformly in [0, 2π) (shaded region: one standard deviation over realizations), the Floquet drive with fixed θn ≡θ, and the Fibonacci drive in which ZZ and X gates alternate according to the Fibonacci substitution rule. The spiral drive exhibits highly reduced entanglement growth compared to any of the other protocols. The system size is L = 400, with h = 0 and ϕz = π/3. (b) SL/2(n) for the spiral drive at n = Fm for different transverse fields h in (7). As h increases, the intermediate entanglement growth goes from logarithmic to linear,...

Figure 4. Plot of Tr(MFn), as defined in (4), shown as a function of the index n for Haar distributed SU(2) matrices P, with 2000 random samples. The averaged value of the trace is shown in blue, and each realization of P is shown as a gray curve. We numerically observe the convergence to the period-6 pattern for all realizations of P.

Figure 5. Deviation of the structure factor from its initial value ex- hibits self-similar revivals. |∆Sxx(k = π/3, n)| = |Sxx(k = π/3, n) −Sxx(k = π/3, 0)|. The locations of the deep dips are essentially independent of L, indicating that the revivals are intrinsic to the quasiperiodic spiral drive.
